nativeRange.habitat: Clear coastal coral reefs in bays and semi-exposed reef edges, generally 3-20 m depth and most often 3-8 m, among living stony corals.
nativeRange.biotope: Live coral cover on shallow tropical reefs.

decorPreferences: established reef with multiple live stony coral colonies
ample live rock with crevices for perching and shelter

diet.notes: The Leopard Blenny is an obligate corallivore that feeds primarily on the superficial coenosarc tissue (the thin living layer that connects coral polyps) of live stony corals. Some individuals have been reported to accept frozen mysis, brine shrimp, chopped crustacean flesh, and herbivore preparations, but most specimens require a steady supply of sacrificial stony coral colonies to survive long-term in captivity.

avoidWith: other combtooth blennies of similar form
any reefkeeper unwilling to sacrifice live stony corals
aggressive fish that would harass it off its perches

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x958. Sharp, vivid pink/red honeycomb spotting across the whole short-bodied fish tucked in Millepora coral; clearly shows the diagnostic 'Pink Spotted' pattern.
Brocken Inaglory; 760x744. Crisp lateral view of the whole fish showing the classic reddish-brown honeycomb spots on a cream body with green head and tail.
Ewout Knoester; ~1024px. Tack-sharp dramatic pose with dorsal and pectoral fins spread, clearly showing leopard spotting and the feathery cirri above the eyes; olive-toned individual for variety.

summary: The Leopard Blenny is an Indo-Pacific combtooth blenny with a spotted body and feathery cirri (tufted projections) above the eyes. Generally considered expert-only, it feeds on the living tissue of stony corals and is difficult to sustain in captivity.
careNotes: The Leopard Blenny is one of the few aquarium-traded fish that is a true obligate corallivore — it feeds on the living tissue of stony corals and is generally considered incompatible with any display reef tank where coral preservation matters. Best reserved for advanced keepers willing to maintain a dedicated tank with a rotating supply of sacrificial coral colonies. A few specimens have been reported to transition onto frozen and prepared foods, but starvation is the most common outcome when offered only standard aquarium fare. Like other combtooth blennies, it has been known to jump, so a tightly fitted lid is generally advised. Generally peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, it tends to be aggressive toward other similar-bodied blennies. For keepers maintaining SPS (small-polyp stony) or LPS (large-polyp stony) coral displays, this fish is effectively not reef-safe and should not be housed with corals intended for preservation.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing. Eggs are demersal and attached to the substrate via an adhesive pad; males prepare nesting sites by overgrazing coral patches. Captive breeding is not routinely accomplished in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference: size 5.5 in, temp 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, min 30 gal, peaceful, reef compatibility 'with caution', care level Expert Only, obligate corallivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy and wild biology: Family Blenniidae, Order Blenniiformes, max 14.5 cm TL, Indo-Pacific (Red Sea to Hawaii and Society Islands, north to Ryukyu/Bonin, south to New Caledonia/Rapa), depth 3-20 m, preferred temperature 24.7-29.3 C (mean 28.3 C), corallivorous, oviparous with distinct pairing.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-check on classification and diet: monotypic genus Exallias, max length 14.5 cm TL, Indo-Pacific incl. Hawaii, Red Sea, Australia and French Polynesia, feeds on superficial coenosarc tissue of coral polyps and has been observed to cause localized coral bleaching.

fish.breedingNotes: Males prepare nesting sites by overgrazing patches of coral and guard the demersal eggs (eggs laid directly on a surface) after spawning. Captive rearing has not been routinely accomplished in home aquariums.
